title: Recursive CTEs in PostgreSQL: Querying Hierarchical Data
excerpt: Learn how to use recursive common table expressions to traverse tree structures like org charts, category trees, and referral chains in PostgreSQL.
content:
## Introduction

Hierarchical data is everywhere: organizational structures, category taxonomies, referral chains, bill-of-materials. The natural representation is a self-referencing table with a `parent_id` column. But querying across multiple levels requires recursion.

PostgreSQL's recursive CTEs let you traverse these trees elegantly in pure SQL, without application-level loops.

## The Table Structure

Consider an employee org chart:

```sql
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    manager_id INTEGER REFERENCES employees(id),
    department TEXT
);
```

## Basic Recursive CTE Pattern

The structure always follows this template:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE cte_name AS (
    -- Base case: the starting point(s)
    SELECT ...
    FROM table
    WHERE condition

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive case: join back to the CTE
    SELECT ...
    FROM table
    JOIN cte_name ON table.parent = cte_name.id
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name;
```

## Example 1: Find All Reports Under a Manager

Get everyone who reports to employee #5, at any depth:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
    -- Base: start with the manager
    SELECT id, name, title, manager_id, 0 AS depth
    FROM employees
    WHERE id = 5

    UNION ALL

    -- Recurse: find direct reports of current level
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.title, e.manager_id, ot.depth + 1
    FROM employees e
    JOIN org_tree ot ON e.manager_id = ot.id
)
SELECT id, name, title, depth
FROM org_tree
WHERE depth > 0  -- exclude the manager themselves
ORDER BY depth, name;
```

The `depth` column tracks how many levels deep each person is. This is invaluable for indented displays or calculating span of control.

## Example 2: Build the Full Path

Generate a breadcrumb path like "CEO > VP Engineering > Team Lead > Developer":

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE path_builder AS (
    -- Base: start from leaf nodes (employees with no reports)
    SELECT
        id,
        name,
        manager_id,
        name::TEXT AS full_path,
        1 AS level
    FROM employees
    WHERE id = 42  -- specific employee

    UNION ALL

    -- Walk UP the tree to the root
    SELECT
        e.id,
        e.name,
        e.manager_id,
        e.name || ' > ' || pb.full_path,
        pb.level + 1
    FROM employees e
    JOIN path_builder pb ON pb.manager_id = e.id
)
SELECT full_path
FROM path_builder
WHERE manager_id IS NULL;  -- reached the root
```

This walks upward from the target employee to the CEO, concatenating names along the way.

## Example 3: Category Tree with Aggregation

For an e-commerce category tree, count products at every level including subcategories:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE category_tree AS (
    SELECT id, name, parent_id, id AS root_id
    FROM categories
    WHERE parent_id IS NULL  -- top-level categories

    UNION ALL

    SELECT c.id, c.name, c.parent_id, ct.root_id
    FROM categories c
    JOIN category_tree ct ON c.parent_id = ct.id
)
SELECT
    ct.root_id,
    cat.name AS top_category,
    COUNT(p.id) AS total_products
FROM category_tree ct
JOIN categories cat ON cat.id = ct.root_id
LEFT JOIN products p ON p.category_id = ct.id
GROUP BY ct.root_id, cat.name
ORDER BY total_products DESC;
```

## Safety: Preventing Infinite Loops

If your data has cycles (a row references itself through a chain), the recursive CTE will run forever. Always add a safety limit:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE safe_tree AS (
    SELECT id, name, manager_id, 1 AS depth,
           ARRAY[id] AS visited  -- track visited nodes
    FROM employees
    WHERE id = 1

    UNION ALL

    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, st.depth + 1,
           st.visited || e.id
    FROM employees e
    JOIN safe_tree st ON e.manager_id = st.id
    WHERE st.depth < 20           -- hard depth limit
      AND e.id != ALL(st.visited) -- prevent cycles
)
SELECT * FROM safe_tree;
```

The `visited` array and depth limit together guarantee termination.

## Key Takeaway

Recursive CTEs turn hierarchical traversal from a multi-query application-level problem into a single, declarative SQL statement. The pattern is always the same: anchor row(s), UNION ALL, self-join on the parent-child relationship. Add a depth counter for safety and display, and you can traverse any tree structure PostgreSQL holds.
